one_line_takeaway: A theory paper arguing that the 'hallucinations are inevitable' computability results are practically vacuous: it proves hallucination probability can be made statistically negligible (via a rote-memorizer existence proof coexisting with inevitability-on-infinite-inputs), so real-world hallucination should be blamed on data or algorithm, not on innate inevitability.
key_finding: There exists a language-model trainer whose hallucinations are (a) UNIFORMLY statistically negligible over the class P_CDF of input distributions whose length-CDF is lower-bounded (given a known input-length CDF lower bound), and (b) NON-UNIFORMLY statistically negligible over ALL distributions on Sigma* (Theorem 21). Hence hallucination probability can be made arbitrarily small with a qualified, sufficiently long training set — coexisting with, and reframing, the inevitability-on-infinitely-many-inputs result. The authors further prove near-optimality: neither the input-length-CDF lower bound (Theorem 27) nor a training-set size exponential in the length threshold (Theorem 29) can be removed by any cleverer algorithm.
core_contribution: A purely theoretical position paper arguing that the 'innate' inevitability of LLM hallucinations established by prior computability-theoretic work (Xu et al. 2024; Banerjee et al. 2024) cannot, in principle, explain the practical hallucination problems of real LLMs. The authors present a contrastive POSITIVE result: within the same discrete-set framework as Xu et al., they prove (Theorem 21 / Proposition 26) that there exists a language-model trainer whose hallucinations are 'statistically negligible' — i.e., the hallucination probability can be driven arbitrarily close to zero given a qualified and sufficiently large training set — even in the worst case over the ground-truth acceptable-output map F0 and the input distribution, and without any assumptions about grammar/semantics. They show this positive result mathematically coexists with the negative inevitability result: a language model can still hallucinate on an INFINITE set of inputs while that set carries arbitrarily small probability. The paper's own restated inevitability result (Theorem 8) is a strengthened, axiom-of-choice-free version of Xu et al.'s Theorems 2 and 3 (F0 made independent of the model h), not a novel inevitability claim. Conclusion: if hallucinations are a practical issue, blame the dataset (quality/quantity) or the algorithm/compute, not diagonal-argument inevitability.
threat_model: N/A — this is a theoretical machine-learning / statistical-learning-theory paper with no adversary, no attack surface, and no security threat model. Its 'worst-case' analysis is a mathematical worst case over the (non-computable) ground-truth acceptable-output map F0 and the input distribution mu, and the no-free-lunch results (Theorems 27, 29) are adversarial only in the mathematical sense of an adversarially chosen F0/mu, not a malicious actor. No threat model should be inferred.
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) The 'qualified' training-data assumption that each output Y_i lies in F0(S_i) with probability 1 is acknowledged as strong (Remark 15). (2) The sufficient training-set size m-bar is exponential in the length threshold and can be 'huge', and Theorem 29 shows such a huge size is unavoidable in this assumption-light framework (Remark 23) — the authors stress this does NOT mean huge data is practically required, only that the general-setting guarantee is essentially as weak as the trivial Rote Memorizer. (3) Only deterministic LMs are analyzed; stochastic LMs and the tension between reducing hallucination and output diversity are left to future work (Remark 4). (4) Theorem 21 is an EXISTENCE result — it shows some (dataset, algorithm) combination exists 'regardless of whether it is realistically preparable', not that a practical recipe exists. (5) The paper itself concludes that to obtain guarantees at practical data sizes one must adopt stronger, natural-language-specific assumptions (Remark 30); it deliberately avoids such assumptions to keep the rebuttal maximally general.

quantitative_results: Sufficient training-set size for uniform statistical negligibility (Rote Memorizer, Prop 26): m-bar = ceil( (|Sigma*|*(n-bar+1) / (1 - CDF(n-bar))) * ln( |Sigma*|*(n-bar+1) / (2*(1 - CDF(n-bar))) ) ), where n-bar satisfies 1 - CDF(n-bar) < (1/2)*min{epsilon_H, epsilon_T}; this bound is exponential in the length threshold n-bar.

No-free-lunch lower bound (Theorem 27, Remark 28): for any trainer and any training size m, in the worst case over F0 and mu the expected hallucination probability E_T[HP] >= 1/2, and Pr_T(HP >= lambda_H) >= lambda_T = (1 - 2*lambda_H)/(2 - 2*lambda_H); e.g. lambda_H = 1/4 gives lambda_T = 1/3 (HP > 1/4 with probability at least 1/3).

Necessity of exponential data (Theorem 29): a training size exponential in a CDF-dependent length parameter is NECESSARY (not just sufficient) for uniform statistical negligibility, matching the Rote Memorizer bound up to the framework's optimality.

Proof-lemma tail bound (Lemma 32 / Appendix C): the failure probability that the memorizer misses a high-probability short string is bounded by |Sigma|^(n+1) * exp( -m*epsilon'_H / (2*|Sigma|^(n+1)) ).
baselines_compared: Conceptual comparison (not empirical) to Xu et al. (2024) — the computability-theoretic inevitability result (any LM hallucinates on infinitely many inputs) that this paper strengthens (Theorem 8) and reframes.
Conceptual comparison to Banerjee et al. (2024) — inevitable-hallucination construction via reduction to the halting problem.
Kalai & Vempala (2024) — inevitability under train/real distribution mismatch, explicitly placed out of scope (this paper assumes qualified data).
The Rote Memorizer (Algorithm 1) is the constructive vehicle for the positive result, not an empirical baseline; the no-free-lunch theorem (Shalev-Shwartz & Ben-David 2014) supplies the lower bounds.
